title: Refrigerant Leak — Low Charge Diagnosis
complaint: System runs but cooling is inadequate. Customer says it has been getting worse over the summer.

s1.prompt: Low suction, low head, high superheat, low subcooling, low amperage. What does this indicate?

s1-wrong.prompt: Overcharge causes HIGH head and HIGH subcooling. Dirty condenser causes HIGH head. All pressures are low — this is undercharge.

s1-partial.prompt: A failed TXV (stuck closed) would cause very low suction and very high superheat. These readings are consistent with undercharge — the TXV is likely responding correctly to low charge.

s2.prompt: Before adding refrigerant, what must you do?

options.label: Add refrigerant immediately to restore charge
Find and repair the leak first

s2-wrong.prompt: Adding refrigerant to a leaking system is a violation of EPA Section 608 and wastes refrigerant. Find and fix the leak first.
s2-wrong.feedback: EPA requires leak repair before recharging systems with 50+ lbs. Even smaller systems should be repaired — not just topped off.

s3.prompt: You use an electronic leak detector and find a leak at a flare fitting on the liquid line. What do you do?

options.label: Tighten the flare fitting and recharge
Recover refrigerant, repair the flare, pressure test with nitrogen, evacuate, then recharge

s3-wrong.prompt: Adding refrigerant without repairing the leak is an EPA violation. The leak must be repaired first.

s3-partial.prompt: Tightening without recovery risks venting refrigerant. The correct procedure is to recover first, then repair, test, evacuate, and recharge.

pass.prompt: Correct. Low charge from a leaking flare fitting. Proper procedure: recover → repair → nitrogen pressure test → evacuate to 500 microns → recharge by subcooling.
pass.feedback: Never add refrigerant to a leaking system. Always recover, repair, test, evacuate, and recharge.
